figures.caption: Figure 4. Training loss curve of VAG over time on the AgiBot dataset for 40,000 iterations.
Figure 5. Qualitative video generation results across different methods. Prompt:“Use the right hand to pour the water from the gray teapot into the cup.” CP2 stands for Cosmos-Predict2.
Figure 6. Action curve of all 16 dimensions (predicted vs GT) over time on a grasping task of the AgiBot dataset. Prompt: “Use the right hand to pick up the apple from the table”.
Figure 8. Training loss curve of VAG over time on the LIBERO dataset for 20,000 iterations.

Figure 7. Visualization of the generated videos and actions in the simulation environment of LIBERO. Prompt:“Pick up the black bowl from table center and place it on the plate.” Top to bottom: generated head-view video; synchronized generated handview video; trajectory replay visualization from the head-view; trajectory replay visualization from the hand-view.

Figure 9. Action curves of all 7 dimensions (predicted vs. ground truth) over time on the spatial task of LIBERO dataset. Prompt: “Pick up the black bowl between the plate and the ramekin and place it on the plate.” The blue curves denote groundtruth actions, while the yellow ones denote predicted actions.

Figure 2. Architecture comparison of embodied models. (a) Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models iteratively predict and execute actions, serving as a policy [6]. (b) World models (WMs) generate rich visual rollouts spanning diverse scenarios but lack aligned action trajectories for direct policy learning [1]. (c) World-Action (WA) models serve as a policy and enhance action prediction by incorporating video generation as an Action-Auxiliary signal [9]. (d) World-Action (WA) models synthesize robot training data by combining world models and IDM [29]. (e) Our framework VAG specializes in joint and aligned video-action generation for embodied data synthesis.
Figure 11. Success rate of VLAs in the real world. Powered by the synthesized data from VAG, π0.5-w-VAG-pretrain showcases better generalization than π0.5 (a 20% increase in success rate).
Figure 3. Inference and training pipeline of VAG. Both the video branch and the action branch are based on flow matching.

Figure 12. Representative examples of VAG-generated video and action trajectories for left-arm, right-arm, and bimanual manipulation. Each example includes (1) the initial VAG input frame and generated video, (2) the textual prompt and action trajectory, and (3) the action trajectory executed on the Agilex robot.
